title: Dog Swelling Under Eye: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's face swollen? Learn about common causes like tooth abscesses and when to see a vet for swelling under the eye.
content_html:
Understanding Swelling Under Your Dog's Eye

Noticing a bump or puffiness under your dog’s eye can be very concerning for any pet owner. This symptom typically points to localized inflammation or infection. Common culprits include a tooth root abscess (specifically the upper carnassial tooth), a bug bite or sting, a clogged tear duct, or even a foreign object stuck in the skin. Because the tissue near the eye is sensitive and thin, swelling can appear rapidly and look quite dramatic.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for swelling under the eye is considered Medium. In most cases, it is not an immediate life-threatening crisis, but it does require professional attention within 24 to 48 hours. If the swelling is accompanied by a sudden loss of vision, a bulging eye, or if your dog is pawing at the area uncontrollably, you should seek care sooner. These issues are often painful, and delaying treatment can lead to the infection spreading or damage to the eye itself.

Why Taking a Photo Helps

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, well-lit photo of the swelling can be incredibly helpful for triage. A photo allows the vet to see the exact location and shape of the lump, which helps differentiate between a skin-related issue and a dental-related one. Furthermore, taking a series of photos over a few hours allows you to track whether the swelling is expanding rapidly, providing vital diagnostic information for your pet's care team.

faqs.question: Can a bad tooth cause swelling under a dog's eye?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is one of the most common causes. An infection in the root of the large upper premolar (carnassial tooth) often drains upward, resulting in a painful lump or even an open sore just below the eye.

faqs.question: What should I do if the swelling starts to drain fluid?
faqs.answer: If you see pus or blood, gently wipe it away with a clean, damp cloth, but do not apply any ointments. Contact your vet immediately as this confirms an infection that likely requires antibiotics.

faqs.question: Could it be an allergic reaction?
faqs.answer: It is possible. If your dog was recently outside or tried a new food, they might have a localized reaction. However, allergic reactions often involve swelling of the muzzle or ears as well, rather than just one side under the eye.
